summarization.a: The spectra chart shows RBS spectrum with backscattered-ion counts versus energy for a zirconium-containing film on a carbon substrate, with features attributed to C (substrate), O, and Zr.
summarization.b: The spectra chart shows RBS spectrum with backscattered-ion counts versus energy for a hafnium-containing film on a carbon substrate, with features attributed to C (substrate), O, and Hf.

data_extraction.a:
| Energy (MeV) | Counts | Assignment |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | - |
| 0.098 | 1273 | Carbon Substrate |
| 0.46 | 443 | Carbon Substrate |
| 0.7 | 318 | Oxygen |
| 1.65 | 3987 | Zirconium |
| 2 | 0 | - |
data_extraction.b:
| Energy (MeV) | Counts | Assignment |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | - |
| 0.098 | 2096 | Carbon Substrate |
| 0.46 | 578 | Carbon Substrate |
| 0.7 | 253 | Oxygen |
| 1.65 | 11240 | Hafnium |
| 2 | 0 | - |

a.question: How does the shape of carbon substrate signal differ from the shape of the metal signals?
a.answer: The carbon substrate signal appears as a broad, continuous signal, while the metal signals appear as sharp, narrow peaks.

a.question: What evidence from the spectra supports the conclusion that the surface contains metal, and the bulk contains carbon?
a.answer: Atoms deeper in the sample produce lower-energy backscattered ions and appear as broad peaks at low energies. Atoms near the surface produce higher-energy signals and appear as sharp peaks. The metal signals appear as sharp, narrow peaks at high energies, confirming that the metal exists as a thin film located strictly at the surface. Conversely, the carbon signal appears as a broad, continuous peak at lower energies, which is characteristic of a bulk material.

a.question: Is there evidence for impurities in the sample, based on this spectrum?
Why does the hafnium peak have different intensity  than the zirconium peak?
a.answer: The hafnium peak is much taller than the zirconium peak due to the scattering cross-section. The probability of an alpha particle scattering off a nucleus increases with the square of the atomic number Z. Hafnium (Z = 72) has  a higher atomic number than zirconium (Z = 40), so it scatters the ions more strongly and produces a more intense signal.
